nativeRange.habitat: Large rivers, flooded forests, and connected lakes of the Amazon basin and associated drainages

diet.notes: Red-Bellied Piranhas are opportunistic feeders in the wild, consuming fish, invertebrates, insects, nuts, seeds, and fruits. In captivity, frozen large prawns, silversides, krill, mussels, and strips of fish flesh are generally recommended. Live feeder fish carry a disease transmission risk and are best avoided, as are mammalian and avian meats.

imageCandidates.notes: H. Zell (Llez), 6000x4000. Tack-sharp full lateral profile; textbook silvery-gold speckled flanks, vivid red-orange belly and throat, red anal fin and red eye all crisp.
Gregory Moine, 4288x2848. Sharp, vibrant orange-red belly on the focal fish with two more piranhas in frame showing the schooling context.
FF23-fr (Stuttgart), 3888x2592. Dramatic dark-water shot with glittering gold speckling and glowing red belly, group visible behind.

summary: The Red-Bellied Piranha (Pygocentrus nattereri) is a well-known Amazon predator whose fearsome reputation tends to be overstated — in captivity these fish are often cautious and best kept in schools of five or more. A large tank, specialist care, and legal verification are all required.
careNotes: LEGAL NOTICE: Pygocentrus nattereri is illegal to own in many US states (including Alabama, Alaska, Arizona, Arkansas, California, Colorado, Florida, Georgia, Hawaii, Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, Montana, Nevada, New Jersey, New Mexico, New York, North Carolina, North Dakota, Oklahoma, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, Virginia, Washington, and others) and in numerous countries. Always verify local regulations before acquiring. The infamous 'piranha frenzy' reputation is largely exaggerated. In the wild, these fish tend to be cautious scavengers, though they are opportunistic predators capable of rapid feeding when prey is available. Keeping them in groups is strongly recommended — a lone specimen tends to be stressed and more prone to aggression, while groups of five or more generally establish dominance hierarchies and settle into calmer behavior. The jaws and teeth are powerful and sharp, so caution during tank maintenance is strongly advised. Heater guards are recommended, as adults have been known to damage submerged equipment. High-turnover filtration (4–5x tank volume per hour) and regular large water changes (30–50% weekly) are needed to maintain water quality. Bare-bottom or standard substrate is acceptable. Live feeder fish are best avoided due to the risk of disease transmission. The 50 cm maximum length sometimes reported refers to a wild record outlier; aquarium specimens generally reach 25–35 cm.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported in captivity. Males are reported to create substrate pits; eggs typically hatch within 2–3 days. Sexual maturity tends to occur around 1 year at approximately 10–15 cm. Fry become increasingly cannibalistic as they grow and have been known to require separation by size class.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish
primary.notes: Primary source used after Aquarium Co-Op returned 404 for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size, temperature, pH, and taxonomy. FishBase reports max 50 cm (wild record outlier); SeriouslyFish hobby range of 25–35 cm used for adultSizeCm. Temperature averaged: SeriouslyFish 24–28°C, FishBase 23–27°C → {min:24, max:28}. pH 5.5–7.5 agreed exactly.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported but requires a dedicated species tank and size-grading fry to prevent cannibalism.
